data.title: Novo Nordisk: Undervalued Pharma Heavyweight With Upside Potential
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NYSE:NVO) is a Danish pharma heavyweight currently trading at undervalued levels, presenting an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. Together with Eli Lilly, the company has a dominant market position in the weight-loss treatment industry. NVO is a growing business operating at high margins.

Novo Nordisk shares fell by around 60% in the last 12 months, indicating a sell-off could be overdone. At a forward P/E of approximately 14, the company is trading around 40% cheaper than an average S&P 500 company, and not all are growing top and bottom-line figures over 20% in recent years.

The Danish healthcare giant operates in an industry expected to grow significantly in the years ahead; if the company maintains its dominant market share, upside is likely just from increased demand.

I think Novo Nordisk is a Strong Buy. It is one of the largest positions in my portfolio, and I think the stock could double in price over the next 12 to 18 months. Trading at undervalued levels, it presents an attractive opportunity, and with future tailwinds, I think the $110 stock price is not out of reach in the next 12 to 18 months.

Growth Of The Weight Loss Treatment Market Is The Key Tailwind

Novo Nordisk is addressing an important issue. A significant number of people in the world face overweight-related problems, and anti-obesity treatment has never been more in demand. The market had about $15 billion in sales in 2024, while Morgan Stanley has raised their guidance from $105 billion in 2035 to a staggering $150 billion.

If the weight loss market increases 10x, Novo Nordisk, as a market leader, will benefit significantly. Wegovy, the company's weight loss drug, has become synonymous with the treatment, and together with Eli Lilly's Zepbound is shaping the industry. The industry felt saturated with compounders offering alternatives due to supply shock, but with the issue resolved, only FDA-approved treatments will remain available in the market.

The National Institute of Health (NIH) shared statistics showing the trend in obesity and severe obesity, indicating a huge issue in the United States. 2017-2018 data suggested more than 42% of Americans were obese, with around 9% experiencing severe obesity.

Novo Nordisk is a well-known brand in the industry, which has an incredible 71% market share internationally. The company disclosed in its Q2 2025 earnings report that total class growth was around 30%, which is significant. With anti-obesity drugs becoming more popular and available worldwide, NVO's dominant 71% position leaves it well-positioned for long-term growth and success.

The US market share is not disclosed, but the company has reported that its total diabetes market share at around 32%. It is significant, as every third weight-loss drug sold came from Novo Nordisk.

Taking into account the growth outlook for the anti-obesity industry, Novo Nordisk should see significant upside driven by increased demand. The healthcare giant is investing heavily in R&D, implying current products could become more effective or new world-changing products could be introduced in the future.

Novo Nordisk is a market leader that continues to improve. With such a large market share internationally, the company is well-positioned for expected industry growth. It should be reflected in their top and bottom line numbers years ahead.

Oversold And Undervalued

The company is trading as a declining business, not a growth story. With a forward P/E of around 14, it is about 44% cheaper than the sector median and the average S&P 500 company. That is surprising and indicates undervaluation, as with top-line growth of 20.90%, it deserves a higher premium.

Novo Nordisk is expected to grow its revenue by about 17% next year, which would be 118% higher than the 8% industry average. If the market is pricing in saturation in the weight-loss treatment market or a loss of dominance, the expected growth does not support that. Forward EBITDA is expected to grow 18.57%, 130% higher than peers. These are impressive numbers, and the current valuation does not justify them — it feels like the stock is oversold, and weak healthcare sentiment may be bottoming.

If one thinks Novo is solely a growth story without significant bottom-line strength to support valuations, that's not true. EPS grew about 24% year over year, which is 49% higher than the industry average, and the bottom line is not expected to slow with projected EPS growth of 22% year-over-year for FY2025 at 3.86.

Novo Nordisk is growing its bottom line as successfully as its top line. Since 2020, the company has consistently increased profitability, and the current 6-month data suggest Novo is on its way to a strong, double-digit bottom-line growth.

NVO is operating in a high-margin business, which has grown consecutively over the past decade. Margins now appear to be consolidating around 34%, but with a large market share, it should be able to keep the momentum going in net income. On a TTM basis, the company reported nearly $17.5 billion in net income, which is already 20% higher than FY2024's $14 billion.

The company is oversold. The chart below suggests it is trading at historically low P/E levels, which indicates it may be positioned for a rebound. There are not that many companies in the market that could grow 20% annually, report double-digit bottom-line growth, and also trade at their 5-year P/E lows.

Nevertheless, the company is expected to grow its dividends by 13.31% next year, which is 3x compared to the industry's average. On a TTM basis, it grew its dividends by 17% — nearly 3x the sector median.

Novo Nordisk is a strong performer, with both top and bottom-line growth. The company is trading at undervalued levels, suggesting it may be a good time to acquire shares. I think Novo Nordisk will benefit a lot from the expected growth of the anti-obesity market, and while being a dominant force in the weight-loss treatment industry, investors may consider building a larger position while it's trading at historical lows.

Why I think Novo Nordisk May Reach $500 Billion Market Cap

NVO's current market cap is around $240 billion. It used to be around $640 billion in mid-2024, which implies a decline of more than 60%. To put it the perspective, the company's revenue grew more than 20% year-over-year since June 2024, from $9.7 billion to $12.1 billion.

Meanwhile, gross profit increased from about $8.3 billion to $10.1 billion, a nearly 19% rise. I think that the stock price drop doesn't make much sense — even if the markets are forward-looking, I think they mispriced the stock. Regardless of the risks and concerns, the company has been fundamentally growing.

There is a saying that markets can stay irrational longer than one can stay solvent — and that seems the case with Novo. More than half of the market cap has been erased over 12 months, while the company grew top and bottom lines by 20%.

To reach a $500 billion market cap — or my $110 price target — improved sentiment alone would be enough. I think it's irrational that the company is growing nearly twice the average S&P 500 company (20% to an average of 10%), is trading at nearly 50% lower multiple (25 compared to 14). This set-up is rare, and that's why I think it is an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. Thus, if the company were to trade at slightly above the average benchmark company, which would be reasonable because of the higher growth, at around a 28 forward multiple — at projected EPS of 3.90, the company would reach $110 price target.

This is realistic. A forward multiple of 28 would still be well below historical averages, and with the tailwinds ahead, I see no reason why it couldn't be trading at these levels in the future.

Wall Street analysts expect $4.00 EPS for FY2026. This supports my thesis and would require a lower multiple of 26.5x to reach the $110 target. However, I think the market is overly pessimistic — the company, in its recent quarterly announcement, guided operating profit to come at around 10% to 16% for FY2025.

Even though management didn't disclose projected EPS growth for FY2026, operating profit growth suggests analysts' EPS FY2026 estimates may be too conservative, as they imply just 3% bottom-line growth next year. The challenge of a saturated weight-loss market due to compounding products was already a risk last year, but the company still posted strong growth.

Novo Nordisk is a well-known brand, a dominant player in the high-margin weight-loss landscape, and is growing its top and bottom lines at nearly double the average benchmark company. With expectations of the anti-obesity market expanding to $125 billion at the midpoint by 2035, it would be enough for Novo to sustain its market share to benefit greatly from rising demand. While trading at historical lows, it presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, and I think it's only a matter of time before sentiment improves.

Novo Nordisk Appears Undervalued Compared To Eli Lilly

Novo's main competition is another healthcare powerhouse, Eli Lilly. One might assume Novo Nordisk is struggling due to losing market share to LLY or its significant outperformance. However, that's not necessarily the case.

Weight-loss growth stories have faced their challenges over the past year, and concerns around compounding have put pressure on both stocks. Eli Lilly has lost about 25% of its market cap over 12 months, and while it has outperformed Novo, the decline in its stock price has been significant. Both companies largely underperformed the index.

While comparing the companies fundamentally, valuation metrics suggest Novo Nordisk is significantly more attractive as an investment compared to Eli Lilly. For instance, the price-to-cash-flow on a TTM basis indicates that Novo is nearly 5x more undervalued than its American competitor. Realizing that Novo dominates more than two-thirds of the international market segment, that is a huge leverage against LLY.

Metric Novo Nordisk Eli Lilly
P/E GAAP (FWD) 14.07 31.94
EV/Sales (FWD) 5.05 10.84
EV/EBITDA (FWD) 10.38 24.34
Price/Cash Flow (TTM) 12.49 57.90
Dividend Yield (TTM) 3.18% 0.82%

From a profitability perspective, Novo Nordisk appears to be a more attractive investment choice, too. It is operating at a higher gross profit margin than Eli Lilly (about 84% to 82%), while both net income margin and cash from operations indicate Novo's stronger positioning.

Metric Novo Nordisk Eli Lilly
Gross Profit Margin (TTM) 84.26% 82.64%
Net Income Margin (TTM) 35.61% 25.91%
Cash From Operations (TTM) $19.19 billion $10.94 billion

Comparing the two, it's not necessarily Eli Lilly's fault, but its presence adds pressure to Novo's stock. Although I think that LLY is a great investment opportunity for long-term investors, Novo, from a fundamental analysis perspective, appears more undervalued and suggests the downside for its stock price could be more limited compared to its American peer.

Main Risks And Concerns

Despite being highly undervalued, there are a few risks and concerns we should take into consideration. They could affect the stock price, especially in the near term.

In its recent Q2 report, management has cut the guidance for total sales and operating growth. Sales growth was lowered from the expected 13-21% to 8%-14%, while operating growth was expected to be between 16-24% initially, but was lowered to 10-16%. It resulted in a significant drop in the stock price — and if the guidance were cut again, uncertainty could pressure the stock price further.

Contraction in profit margins. For a while now, margins have been consolidating, which presents a risk that they might have peaked. With potential alternatives to Novo's products, the company may be forced to lower prices, which would affect margins.

R&D challenges. To scale anti-obesity treatment, healthcare giants are working to replace injectable drugs with oral products. If LLY succeeds first, Novo could face market share losses, especially in the US, accelerating the sell-off.

Regulatory concerns are always an open risk to healthcare companies. Changes within the weight-loss industry framework could bring uncertainty, and forward-looking markets could pressure dominant players.

The broader stock market has experienced a significant rally from its April lows, which could signal a temporary pause or consolidation. If that happens and sentiment shifts to profit-taking, Novo's stock price could decline alongside the market.

I think investors should watch whether Novo Nordisk can continue growing both the top and bottom lines. If the company succeeds, it presents an incredible opportunity for long-term investors, and purchasing stock at historical lows could potentially lead to significant benchmark outperformance.

Conclusion: Novo Nordisk Is A Strong Buy — A Stock To Hold For Years

I think that Novo Nordisk at current levels is an absolute buy. Therefore, I state a Strong Buy rating with a $110 price target over 12 to 18 months. If the company were to trade at a slight premium compared to a typical S&P 500 company at 25, the price target could even appear conservative.

The business operates in a high-margin landscape and is growing significantly faster than the sector norm. It is highly profitable, consistently invests in R&D, and keeps improving its products. The weight-loss market is expected to increase nearly 10x by 2035, and with 71% international market share, Novo would be one of the main beneficiaries.

11 analysts suggest a one-year average price of $63.57, implying about 15% growth. I am more optimistic — I think if anti-obesity market sentiment improves, it may be enough to drive a significant upside. However, based on the historical growth and management's FY2025 guidance, I think the company will continue expanding its top and bottom lines — and if that is reassured, the market should reward investors. Novo Nordisk has strong tailwinds ahead, making it a compelling investment possibility to consider.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Nvidia Time
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The chipmaker’s numbers could signal if the AI trade still has legs. (0:17) Post-Jackson Hole, the Fed’s favorite inflation gauge hits. (1:54) European post offices pause package shipments to the U.S. (3:19)

The following is an abridged transcript:

The 800 lb. earnings gorilla hoves into view this week as Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) reports earnings on Wednesday.

The consensus for the biggest company on Wall Street is for EPS of $1.01 on revenue of $46 billion. The temporary ban on H20 processors to China that took effect in April is expected to have just a minimal impact on quarterly performance. The Trump administration recently removed the ban in exchange for a 15% slice of sales revenue, which Oppenheimer expects to be offset by price increases.

SA analyst KM Capital, which has a Strong Buy on the stock, says reasons for bullishness ahead of earnings “include accelerating AI tailwinds as well as Nvidia's dominance in the industry. Moreover, Nvidia is a real earnings surprise champion that very rarely misses consensus forecasts.”

The recent pullback “looks like a prime opportunity to buy NVDA before earnings,” they said.

But SA analyst Bears of Wall Street says “here’s a possibility that we’re in the middle of the AI bubble since the capital returns by hyperscalers on AI hardware might yield limited returns, while the current record rate of capital expenditures on AI infrastructure is unsustainable.”

“Considering Nvidia’s great exposure to a handful of customers that generate a significant portion of its sales, there’s a risk that if the bubble pops soon, then the company’s growth opportunities will be limited, while its shares might collapse from the current levels,” they said.

Along with Nvidia, 14 other S&P 500 companies will report earnings this week.

On the calendar:

  • Bank of Montreal (BMO), MongoDB (MDB) and Okta (OKTA) report numbers on Tuesday.
  • Along with Nvidia, Crowdstrike (CRWD), Snowflake (SNOW) and HP (HPQ) weigh in on Wednesday.
  • On Thursday, Dell (DELL), Dollar General (DG), Li Auto (LI), Ulta Beauty (ULTA) and Best Buy (BBY) issue numbers.
  • Alibaba (BABA) reports Friday.

Looking to the economy, traders are adjusting to the post-Jackson Hole macro environment.

Following Fed Chairman Jay Powell’s speech on Friday odds of a September quarter-point rate cut rebounded and are now at 75% (lower than they were immediately post-Powell). Another cut of 25 bps is priced in through the end of the year, with a one-in-four chance of 50 bps of cuts in total.

Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at T.S. Lombard, says: "Surprising to see Powell offer such a clear policy signal with so much data before the next FOMC meeting (and yes, was a clear signal)."

Neil Dutta, head of economics at Renaissance Macro Research, says: "This is about the September Fed meeting. If you get solid economic data, you’ll get a hawkish cut in September. If you get bad data, you’ll get a dovish cut.”

“That’s how to think about this. Through the lens of an insurance cut being green lit in September. Powell did not lay the ground work for a series of rate cuts.”

This Friday, the Fed will get the latest measure of its favorite inflation gauge, the core PCE price index. That arrives with the July income and spending figures.

Economists are expecting a rise of 0.3%. That would boost the annual rate to 2.9%, the highest since February.

Wells Fargo economists say: “Tariff-related price pressures are broadening across the goods sector and appear to be spilling over into the services sector.”

“We ultimately expect core PCE inflation to peak slightly above 3% by the end of the year,” they said. “With inflation drifting in the wrong direction and the labor market losing momentum, the Federal Reserve faces difficult trade-offs in balancing its dual mandate.”

In the news this weekend, lack of clarity on rules about package exemptions is leading to a pause on shipping of packages by a host of European nations to the U.S.

The “de minimus” exemption allows packages worth less than $800 to come into the U.S. duty free. It is set to expire on Friday, August 29.

Postal services in Germany, Denmark, Sweden and Italy said on Saturday they will stop shipping most merchandise to the U.S. effective immediately. France and Austria will follow on Monday. The U.K.'s Royal Mail said it would halt shipments to the U.S. on Tuesday to allow time for those packages to arrive before duties kick in.

Spotify (SPOT) plans further price increases for customers as it invests in new features and pursues a goal of 1 billion users, according to a senior executive.

Alex Norström, co-president and chief business officer, told the Financial Times that price rises were “part of our toolbox now” after many years of keeping them flat.

Spotify is developing features to provide more value and “stickiness” for subscribers. The company recently added a way to customize transitions between tracks on its nearly 9 billion user-created playlists. It has also expanded its services to include audiobooks and podcasts, as well as tools like an AI DJ.

And for income investors, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and S&P Global (SPGI) go ex-dividend on Tuesday. J&J pays out on Sept. 9 and S&P Global pays out on Sept. 10.

Hyatt (H) goes ex-dividend on Wednesday, paying out on Sept. 10.

Tyson Foods (TSN) goes ex-dividend on Friday, with a Sept. 12 payout date.

data.title: JPLD: Continues To Be Attractive In Today's Macro Environment
data.description:
Thesis

In the beginning of the year, we started our coverage of the JPMorgan Limited Duration Bond ETF (JPLD) with a 'Buy' rating. The name has performed since, with a total return in excess of 3.63% since. Given the shift in the macro landscape, we are going to revisit the fund and its analytics and outline why we believe the ETF to still be a solid 'Buy'.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $2.1 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.24%
  • Annualized volatility: 1.85%
  • SEC yield: 4.41%

The name has seen a significant increase in its AUM in the past year:

Since August 2024, the fund has seen a 3x increase in its total assets under management to $2.18 billion currently. That is a very significant move, and indicates investor interest in the product.

Composition - overweight agency mortgages

The ETF is currently overweight agency mortgages, followed by ABS securities:

  • Agency mortgage: 29%
  • ABS: 23%
  • CMBS: 15%
  • Non-agency MBS: 8.5%
  • CLO: 8%
  • Credit: 7%
  • Treasury: 6%
  • Other: residual

The fund has a rate-focused profile, and runs a short duration of 1.8 years, with an average life for its portfolio of 3 years. Targeting the short end of the curve makes this ETF a play on Fed Funds. JPLD is set to gain in value as the Fed cuts rates since the front end of the yield curve has significant correlation to Fed Funds.

The name is very active, with a portfolio turnover rate of 30%, and a very high quality collateral composition:

  • AAA: 73%
  • AA: 13%
  • A: 10%
  • BBB: 4%

The fund therefore takes very little in terms of credit risk, which explains its ultra-low volatility. An investor can almost see this fund as a cash parking vehicle with a duration aspect to it.

Comparison versus its cohort

The fund is the JPMorgan answer to the BlackRock's Short Duration Bond Active ETF (NEAR):

We have also put in the comparison cohort the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC) and the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY). Most of the names here are also overweight agency MBS bonds.

We can see a very similar performance in the cohort in the past year, with a low dispersion in total returns and similar volatility. BINC has had the largest drawdown in the contemplated time period, with a significant move down in April 2025.

In essence, all the above names represent take-on rates via their overweight positioning in Agency MBS, and also short duration vehicles set to gain as the Fed cuts rates. There is an active management aspect to all of them, with managers trying to generate additional yield via actively trading securities and the portfolios.

Macro context

While the market is still debating how many cuts will happen in 2025 and if Powell will be ousted, one thing is certain for 2026, namely lower rates:

The above are the market traded probabilities for rate cuts. The blue boxes indicate where Fed Funds are most likely to end up per Fed meeting given where the market trades futures currently. One thing is for certain, and that is lower Fed Funds by 100 bps in 2026. As we can see from the able above, the blue boxes for mid-2026 are squarely in the 325-250 bps range. Also kindly remember that the current Fed chair mandate expires in May 2026, and President Trump will nominate another chair. It is not a secret for anybody that President Trump wants much lower rates, so one can only assume the new chair will have a similar view on the topic.

Lower rates are coming with a 1-year horizon, although the market is still debating the timing. JPLD is a fund that will benefit directly from lower Fed Funds via its composition and duration profile.

Risk factors

As we have seen from the fund composition, there is very little credit risk here. Even if a recession comes, JPLD should not really suffer a drawdown higher than -1.5%. The main risk, in our view, is if rates stay elevated. If that happens, the fund transforms itself into a dividend vehicle, and the 30-day SEC yield here is not all that appealing. The fund is to be bought with expectations of lower rates. So in a flat rate environment, the name might perform more in-line with money market funds. In our view, that is not a bad risk factor to have all considered.

Conclusion

JPLD is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name has a short duration of 1.8 years and represents a play on short rates via its overweight Agency MBS position. The fund runs almost no credit risk and has a very low annualized volatility that ends up sub 2%. The ETF has seen massive growth in AUM in the past year, now surpassing $2 billion in assets. The fund is set to benefit from lower Fed Funds and represent a very low-volatility way to take advantage of the coming monetary easing environment. We like JPLD and consider it to continue to be a 'Buy' in today's macro context.

data.title: Nvidia's Earnings Report Looks Like A Huge Gamble
data.description:
All eyes will turn to Nvidia (NVDA) this week, as investors hold their collective breath. For the most part, it is easy to argue that the health of the market is dependent on the health of Nvidia. The question isn't whether Nvidia will have solid results. The question is whether the results will be good enough to meet the high expectations, given its massive run-up.

However, based on the revenue and guidance history, as well as the current options positioning, playing this quarter's results looks like a huge gamble.

Revenue Trends

Revenue in the fiscal second quarter is expected to increase by 52.8% to $45.9 billion, with earnings rising by 47.7% to $1.00 per share, and gross margins of 72.1%. Analysts forecast fiscal third-quarter revenue to be $52.7 billion at the midpoint of the range, with margins expected to be 73.3%.

By now, everyone is probably aware that Nvidia tends to low-ball its guidance and then exceed the upper end of that range by $1 to $2 billion every quarter. Although last quarter, the beat was much smaller, at just $202 million.

We also know that the company aims for revenue to grow by $4 to $5 billion per quarter sequentially, which is something they have achieved since the third quarter of 2023. Although analysts expect the company to grow revenue by just $1.8 billion this quarter, compared to last quarter, it is expected to make up for that lack of growth in the fiscal third quarter by guiding fiscal third quarter revenue to $52.7 billion. However, if the historical pattern of growing by $4 to $5 billion continues in the fiscal second quarter, the company should beat analysts' estimates and report revenue around $48 billion.

We also know that Nvidia typically provides guidance that is $4 to $5 billion above the upper end of the range of the previous quarter's guidance. This trend has been evident since the fiscal third quarter of 2023; however, last quarter, the streak was broken, with a $2.0 billion increase over the prior quarter. If the company returns to its old ways, it should be able to guide revenue to between $50 billion and $51 billion. Which is where the problem starts, because the sell-side is looking for revenue for $52.7 billion, which means the company needs to guide the range to $52 to $53.5 billion for the mid-point range to meet anaylsts' estimates, that would be almost $7 billion more than last quarter's guidance at the upper end of the range. In fact, with consensus already near $53 billion, the company may need to guide investors to $53 to $55 billion to truly surprise them, or $8 billion above the prior quarter, which is about double its historical trends.

It isn't to say it can't be done; it is just noting that it is well outside the norm of what the company has been delivering for nearly two straight years.

Option Positions

Options positioning in Nvidia, as usual, is bullish going into the results. The biggest concentration of option gamma is at $180 for expiration on August 29, with the call wall at $185, and the put wall at $170. That means, unless Nvidia reports results that significantly exceed estimates and come in above historical norms, it will have a tough time surpassing $185, while being supported at around $170. These are likely to change somewhat as we approach the earnings report time.

Meanwhile, total delta values in the stock are positive and bullish. Unless the stock clears the call wall at $185, once the event risk passes and implied volatility drops, there will be calls to be unwound, which will put downward pressure on the shares. Basically, at this point, a $180 call with a premium paid of $5.28 won't be able to profit if the stock can't clear the call wall, if held until Friday.

This means that Nvidia will not only have to maintain its historical performance from a reported revenue standpoint but also provide guidance that exceeds its historical norms. It isn't to say that it can't be done, but as investors, we try to make educated decisions on outcomes that we think have some logic to them; otherwise, all we are doing is gambling.

This one clearly appears to be a gamble if you choose to place a bet on the stock rising significantly after it reports results. The company will need to report one of its best quarters ever and provide guidance that exceeds its past performance by almost double.

Nothing is impossible, but sometimes the best trade is the trade not made.

data.title: Devon Energy: Mispriced Upstream Play For Deep Value Investors
data.description:
DVN's Discounted Valuations & Efficient Capital Spending Trends Trigger Deep-Value Buy Opportunity

We previously covered Devon Energy (DVN) in May 2025, discussing its interlinked position to the volatile oil/gas spot prices, which had already triggered the moderating stock prices, as similarly observed in its energy peers.

With the numerous macro-events triggering near-term uncertainties surrounding the global oil demand/supply balance, the producer had attempted to balance the decline in spot prices with expanded production outputs while lowering their capital costs.

Combined with its undeniably discounted valuations, we had rated the stock as a buy for those seeking its eventual upward re-rating while being paid to wait with decent forward dividend yields of 3%.

DVN 1Y Stock Price

Since then, DVN has been consistently climbing by +8.1% compared to the wider market's +7.5%, with a similar recovery also observed in its oil/gas peers in varying degrees.

Much of their tailwinds are naturally attributed to the recovering Brent oil spot prices to $67s ranges by the time of writing, up from the $60s ranges recorded in May 2025, with it apparent that market sentiments surrounding the energy sector remain quite optimistic thus far.

This is despite the OPEC+ agreement on "accelerated output hikes" from September 2025 onwards, with it building upon the projected increase in the US' crude oil production from 12.3M barrels per day in 2019 and 13.2M barrels per day in 2024, to approximately a peak of 13.6M barrels per day by December 2025 and another 13.3M barrels per day expected in 2026.

These are material developments indeed, since the US Energy Information Administration already expects the global oil inventory builds to exceed 2M barrels per day in Q4'25 and Q1'26, with it naturally triggering the imbalanced demand supply trends and consequently, a further suppression in the commodities' spot prices ahead.

Even so, we are not overly concerned about the ongoing volatility, since it is inherent to the energy sector with the fluctuations along with the macroeconomic/geopolitical environment remaining key risks to their investment thesis, DVN included.

Here is where DVN has executed brilliantly in our opinion, since the independent oil/gas company has been able to report improved capital efficiency at $932M in FQ2'25 (-3.3% QoQ/+4.7% YoY) while delivering higher production outputs at 841 MBoe/d (+3.1% QoQ/+18.9% YoY).

This is on top of still generating rich H1'25 cash flows at $1.59B (+11.1% YoY) - with it underscoring its compelling energy investment story as the management continue:

  • paying out fixed dividends (with variable payouts temporarily suspended),
  • retiring -2.3% of its float since FQ4'24 (after the dilutive acquisition of the Williston Basin business of Grayson Mill Energy), and
  • deleveraging their balance sheet to a net debt of -$7.11B (-13.2% from FQ3'24 levels of $8.2B).

For now, DVN investors may want to temper their expectations first, since the $2.5B debt reduction target, $1B of term loan repayment, and the management's FY2025 Free Cash Flow guidance at $3B (assuming that WTI remains at $65s) imply that its variable dividends are likely to be non-existent for least a few more quarters, or the third quarter of 2026 as hinted by the management.

This is the point of variable dividends, in our opinion, allowing the management to utilize numerous levers to drive growth through acquisition and/or intensified capex, while balancing shareholder returns.

This is especially since the moderating spot prices have triggered headwinds to DVN's upstream execution, particularly the NGL prices by -18.7% QoQ/-9.5% YoY and the gas prices by -37% QoQ/+41.8% YoY in FQ2'25, as these comprises 53.9% of its overall production (+1.3 points YoY).

While the EIA is expecting the NGL spot prices to increase by +19.4% YoY in 2026, attributed to the higher exports, the overall net effect is likely to be neutral at best, attributed to the projected moderation in oil prices by -23.8% YoY and gas spot prices by -6.4% YoY.

So, Is DVN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DVN 6Y Stock Price

DVN's 6Y price chart has been discouraging indeed, to put it optimistically, with it further reinforcing its underwhelming investment thesis thus far.

DVN Valuations

Even so, thanks to the stock price underperformance, there is no denying DVN's extremely cheap FWD P/E valuations of 8.40x and FWD Price/Cash Flow valuations of 3.24x, compared to the sector median of 12x/5.27x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 15.21x/15.09x, respectively, with it underscoring its potentially rich capital appreciation upon normalization.

Naturally, this is assuming that an upward re-rating actually occurs, since DVN has lagged many of its upstream and diversified oil/gas peers, as similarly observed in its tepid stock price performance over the past year, including:

  • ConocoPhillips (COP) - upstream - at FWD P/E valuations of 14.77x and FWD Price/Cash Flow valuations of 5.92x,
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY) - up/midstream, at 19.18x/4.17x,
  • Exxon Mobil (XOM) - diversified - at 16.17x/8.54x, and
  • Chevron (CVX) - diversified - at 19.59x/8.33x, respectively.

These developments underscore why DVN's discounted valuations may have been attributed to its undeniably increased performance risks arising from the volatile spot prices, albeit still not explaining the gap to COP as upstream energy companies.

This is especially since DVN is still cheap with an Enterprise Value to Proven Reserve ratio of 13.53x, based on its Enterprise Value of $29.10B at the time of writing and the 2024 proved reserves of 2.15M BOE (+18.6% YoY), compared to COP at 17.48x, based on $136.56B and 7.81M BOE, respectively.

Combined with the uncertainty surrounding the reinstatement of DVN's variable dividends (fixed at $0.24 and variable unknown), against its peers' fixed dividend payout cadence, we can understand why dividend hunters have looked elsewhere, based on the former's lower forward dividend yields of 2.84% against COP at 3.29%, OXY at 2.17%, XOM at 3.65%, and CVX at 4.41%.

Here is where we believe that opportunistic investors may opt for the dollar cost average while waiting for DVN's upcoming reversal by +29.2% to our base-case long-term price target of $43.80, based on the FWD P/E valuations of 8.40x and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $5.22.

Otherwise, assuming an upward re-rating nearer to its upstream peer at 14x (not too far from the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 15x), we are looking at an extremely rich, more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case price target of $73.

These imply DVN's compelling investment thesis for value-oriented investors, especially since the reinstatement of its variable dividends is only a matter of time, as observed in the ongoing deleveraging cadence.

These reasons are also why we are reiterating our Buy rating.

data.title: HODL Waives Sponsorship Fees Until January 2026
data.description:
The VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) is a strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the performance of the price of bitcoin. The strategy directly holds bitcoin, providing investors with exposure to the cryptocurrency spot price through an exchange-traded fund wrapper.

About the Fund

HODL was launched on January 4, 2024, as a bitcoin spot ETF. The strategy directly holds bitcoin in order to provide investors with direct exposure to the cryptocurrency, allowing the strategy to track the spot price. HODL is indexed to the MarketVector Bitcoin Benchmark Rate [BBR].

HODL has $1.9b in assets under management [AUM] with an average of 850k shares changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors and traders with substantial liquidity with minimal spread risk, or cost-in/cost-out at 0.04%. HODL has 17mm bitcoin within the trust, equating to 283 bitcoin per 1,000 shares.

The ETF sponsor has waived all fees from November 2024 through January 10, 2026, for assets upwards of $2.5b in the trust. Beyond the target assets or following the term date, HODL will charge a 20bps sponsor fee on assets under management.

HODL takes a unique approach to holding bitcoin, storing the assets in cold storage with a qualified custodian. Cold storage is the act of storing bitcoin on physical hardware that is not connected to the Internet in order to ensure the security of the bitcoin. Other bitcoin ETFs that undergo this methodology of storage include CoinShare Bitcoin ETF (BRRR) and ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB). By comparison, HODL may be appealing for investors seeking bitcoin exposure as the sponsor fees are currently being waived. Net of fees, each of these strategies is expected to perform in tandem as the only assets being invested in are bitcoin. With no differentiation across strategies, the best next thing would be to target the lowest fee strategy.

Aside from fees, a major differentiator may be assets under management and fund flows. Many of the bitcoin ETFs have remained relatively stagnant since launching at the beginning of 2024 in terms of net asset growth, while BlackRock (IBIT) has consistently grown above all others. Given that bitcoin ETFs may be used for liquidity in the market, ETF liquidity may be just as important as investing in a low-fee strategy.

From a tactical perspective, HODL is directionally correlated with the S&P 500 (SPX) to a certain degree. Some estimate that bitcoin has an 83% correlation to the SPX. Given the institutionalization of bitcoin, I believe that the cryptocurrency should be expected to move similarly to the broader equity index, particularly when considering the broad adoption of bitcoin ETFs across retirement portfolios. Given that bitcoin can be utilized as a liquid asset for trading & cash raises, I suspect that the security and related ETFs may continue to directionally move with the broader equity index.

Interestingly enough, bitcoin faced some selling pressure following recent news of Cleveland Federal Reserve President Beth Hammack not supporting an interest rate cut. Though I believe it may be far-reaching correlating the two events, the relationship between US rate policy and the price of bitcoin may suggest that bitcoin isn’t as decentralized as one may suspect given the relative pricing in relation to the US Dollar.

The bitcoin strategy also exhibits some correlation to the 10-year treasury rate, suggesting that the price of bitcoin may be more tied to US rates than one may have expected.

In terms of the growth trajectory for bitcoin, Bernstein analysts expect bitcoin’s rally to continue into 2026 and potentially into 2027, expecting bitcoin to run up to $200,000/BTC. Part of the optimism is in relation to the Trump administration’s general support for the market with the passage of the GENIUS Act as well as the establishment of the Federal Bitcoin Reserve.

Risks Related to Bitcoin ETFs

Bitcoin ETFs are single-asset investment strategies that hold no diversification or differentiation net of fees and liquidity. Given the popularity of the investment, bitcoin ETFs may pose an investment concentration risk in which the asset is so widely and heavily invested in that it could potentially pose certain risks if a sell-off were to occur.

The establishment of a Federal Bitcoin Reserve could potentially lead to substantially more government control over the cryptocurrency and may lead to monetary policy directed at bitcoin. Though this would be a far-reaching presumption, I believe that there could be the possibility of this occurring when considering the potential for bitcoin to be bought and sold to cover the national debt level.

There have been recent discussions of BitBond issuances, or bitcoin-linked Treasury bonds, to help alleviate the debt burden of the US Federal Government. The premise of these BitBonds is to guarantee a 1% annual coupon in USD with a bitcoin-linked payout at maturity.

Final Thoughts

HODL may be an appealing bitcoin ETF given the fee waiver through January 2026, providing investors with a bitcoin exposure without the management fee. I am recommending HODL with a buy rating.

data.title: Outlook Therapeutics: Why The Odds Point To A Favorable FDA Decision
data.description:
Thesis

With the FDA decision on ONS-5010 (Lytenava) just days away, Outlook Therapeutics (OTLK) sits at a true inflection point for me. Keep in mind that this drug is already validated in Europe and the UK, with sales underway and a decade of market exclusivity. It gives the company a proven regulatory path and a first-mover advantage as the only approved ophthalmic bevacizumab. As you know, the resubmission package to the FDA addresses prior manufacturing concerns. However, I think Outlook's resubmission is steady enough to overcome them, as I'll explain. Also, the NORSE EIGHT trial provides a supportive clinical and safety dataset that regulators in Europe have already deemed sufficient for approval. So now investors just need the FDA to follow suit.

Given these factors, I believe the probability of a favorable US outcome is high, and with a relatively small market cap, approval could act as a powerful re-rating catalyst. In my view, this is a rare asymmetric setup in biotech. On one hand, the downside is clear, but the upside could be transformational. For investors with a small risk appetite, I think this is the moment to take a bullish stance on Outlook ahead of the FDA decision. Shares can be picked up in the mid-$2 range and carry a lot of momentum going into next week.

Why Outlook's Stock Is Soaring

Outlook is a pretty small biopharmaceutical company focused on eye health. Particularly, they look at diseases that can cause vision loss, like wet age-related macular degeneration (wet AMD), diabetic macular edema [DME], and retinal vein occlusion. For those who aren't aware, diseases like these can be pretty devastating because they directly affect the retina, the part of the eye that processes light and then sends signals to the brain. When these conditions progress, people, of course, lose their central vision. It makes it tough to read, drive, recognize faces, or even handle day-to-day tasks. So, if left untreated, they can lead to permanent vision loss.

Now, Outlook's big project in regards to this is Lytenava, which is essentially a specially formulated version of bevacizumab, a drug that's already widely used in cancer care but has also been repurposed by doctors off-label to treat retinal diseases. The catalyst here is that Outlook wants to bring an FDA-approved and globally-approved ophthalmic version of this treatment to market, rather than just relying on compounded versions that aren't officially licensed for eye injections.

So right now, their main revenue source, and almost their only source, is tied to Lytenava. In Europe and the UK, they've already secured approvals, but in the US, they're waiting on the FDA's decision. If the drug clears that hurdle, the company could start generating a lot more revenue through direct sales and distribution partnerships. Until then, we shouldn't view Outlook as a revenue-heavy business but more towards that "pipeline-to-market" stage. So, as with many other small pharma companies, their financial future really hinges on successfully commercializing Lytenava in the US and expanding its use globally.

The market already knows it and is factoring in a favorable FDA outcome on August 27th. The stock is up 76% over the last 6 months. However, Outlook already has a successful drug campaign in place across Europe; it's a campaign that could translate directly over to the US if an approval comes. That's what gives me more confidence in the future of the stock.

Financials

Outlook Therapeutics is in an early commercialization phase, and the financials definitely reflect that. For the latest quarter, revenue came in at just $1.5 million, entirely from the initial rollout of Lytenava in the UK and Europe. They run at a net loss and are cash-flow negative, relying heavily on capital raises to fund operations. This is typical for a small biotech like Outlook. But US approval could quickly shift this financial picture by unlocking access to that much larger American market. Without it, the company's reliance on external financing will remain a major overhang.

As of the latest earnings, Outlook held just $8.9 million in cash and equivalents, down roughly 40% from $14.9 million at the end of 2024. The company incurred a net loss of $20.2 million, meaning it spent more than double its current cash position in just three months. At this pace, I estimate the company has less than one quarter of the runway left, posing a pressing need for additional capital.

Lytenava Updates

Their lead, and really only, drug candidate is Lytenava. As I said before, it's a formulation of bevacizumab, a drug that has long been used in oncology. But here's where I like where Outlook is going with it. It's re-engineered to meet the strict standards required for ophthalmic use. In theory, Lytenava works by blocking vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), a protein that drives the growth of those abnormal, leaky blood vessels in the retina we don't want to see in patients. So by shutting down VEGF, the drug helps stop fluid buildup and scarring that lead to vision decline in the conditions I mentioned. Now, on the regulatory front, Lytenava already has a strong foothold in Europe. The European Commission approved it back in May 2024. Back then, they granted it a decade of market exclusivity, and the UK's MHRA approval followed shortly after in July 2024.

In December 2024, NICE also recommended it for use in the UK's National Health Service, which was a great sign; it somewhat cleared a path for real-world uptake. Sales began in Germany, the UK, and other EU countries in 1H2025. In fact, we already saw those sales show up on earnings, with the company pulling in about $1.5 million in revenue. It positioned Lytenava as the first approved ophthalmic bevacizumab in those markets. That first-mover advantage matters a lot in my eyes. Until now, doctors have just relied on repackaged versions of Avastin for eye injections, which were never actually formally approved for ophthalmic use. Those off-label injections pose some quality and contamination risks, especially in Germany, which alone accounts for over half of Europe's 2.8 million anti-VEGF injections each year. A partnership with Cencora supports market access and logistics across the EU and UK.

As usual, in the US, and with the FDA, things are less straightforward. Outlook resubmitted its license application to the FDA in February this year after addressing earlier concerns, particularly around manufacturing controls, which the FDA is usually very strict on. It's what's known as chemistry, manufacturing, and controls. We have to remember this isn't a pill you swallow; the drug is injected directly into the eye. So the FDA is extra strict about sterility and quality. Outlook had to demonstrate its whole processes for aseptic production, which include sterilizing filtration, so rigorous environmental monitoring in its facilities. The FDA also looks closely at particulate matter, protein aggregation, and for any silicone oil droplets that could cause inflammation or damage inside the eye. On top of that, Outlook needed to provide validated potency assays. They essentially show how each vial consistently neutralizes VEGF, as well as stability data proving the drug, of course, holds up during storage and shipment. Since this will have to be shipped overseas. The packaging and container system itself also comes under scrutiny to ensure there's zero leaching of harmful materials and that the cold-chain requirements, about 2 to 8 °C and light protection, are rock solid.

An FDA Resubmission

The clinical backbone of the FDA resubmission is the NORSE EIGHT trial. It's a Phase 3 study in 400 treatment-naïve wet AMD patients. They compared monthly injections of Lytenava against ranibizumab over about 90 days. Now, what they were looking for was non-inferiority in vision gains at Week 8. But on that strict endpoint, the trial came up short. However, the good news is that at both Day 30 and Day 90, Lytenava's results did meet the non-inferiority thresholds, and anatomical measures like central foveal thickness moved in the right direction.

So in short, the drug didn't tick all the FDA's main boxes at the 8-week mark, but I liked the broader dataset a lot. It showed it works in line with an established competitor. Safety is another piece where the FDA weighs heavily. But across the trial, I thought the safety profile looked consistent with what's expected from anti-VEGF injections. Rates of ocular side effects, like any conjunctival hemorrhage, inflammation, or even transient increases in intraocular pressure, were well in line with ranibizumab. Serious events such as stroke or heart attack were extremely rare and balanced between both groups, but most importantly, no cases of retinal vasculitis were reported. The thing is, regulators in Europe have already codified these risks into Lytenava's approved label, with detailed instructions on aseptic preparation and the use of a filter needle to minimize particulates. In Europe, they also monitor patients for post-injection pressure spikes, which is very normal to do.

So let's bring this all together for the upcoming FDA approval. The cleaned-up manufacturing package, the supportive NORSE EIGHT data, and the established safety record will form the backbone of Outlook's February 2025 BLA resubmission. The FDA has, of course, accepted it and set a decision deadline in 4 days' time. If approval comes through, Lytenava would become the first FDA-approved ophthalmic bevacizumab. The fact that the drug is in use in Europe with all these safety regulations in place makes me bullish on the outcome of the FDA decision at the end of this month. Since Outlook already has that revenue stream in place in Europe, an FDA approval would allow them to translate the drug directly over to the US market. It would move the stock in a very positive direction. The market is betting on an approval, but what they're not factoring in is that the drug is already launched overseas. So we could see revenue for the drug show up a lot quicker than investors realize.

What's On The Table

The US right now represents the largest addressable market for anti-VEGF therapies. Some analysts estimated a total global annual spend somewhere in the range of $13 billion. Last year, the US topped the spending in the North American anti-VEGF therapeutics market, with revenue of about $6.7 billion. That would be across wet AMD, DME, and RVO treatments. So, if Lytenava secures FDA approval, it would become the first officially sanctioned ophthalmic bevacizumab. It would give them a nice positioning to capture a meaningful share from compounded Avastin, which currently accounts for a sizable portion of injections due to its lower cost compared with branded drugs like Eylea and Lucentis. Even a modest penetration, say, 5% of the US anti-VEGF injection market, could translate into hundreds of millions in annual revenue for Outlook. A company with just $1.5 million in revenue last quarter. Importantly, we should keep in mind that the company is not starting from scratch on manufacturing; its CMC and supply chain infrastructure have already been validated through its European approvals and ongoing sales. That means it could begin scaling US distribution relatively quickly post-approval. To do this, they would leverage their Cencora partnership to handle logistics and market access.

Risks

The big risk with the upcoming FDA decision is that Outlook is essentially an all-in bet on Lytenava. It's a bet that has already been priced into the stock. The market is anticipating a positive outcome. If the FDA raises new concerns, whether about the clinical data from NORSE EIGHT or any of the manufacturing controls that tripped them up before, the approval could be delayed again or even denied. That would likely hit the stock hard since it has run up 28% over the past month. On top of that, Outlook is still burning cash and has a history of raising money through share dilution, so a negative outcome could force another capital raise at some very unfavorable prices.

Valuation Of The Stock

Market cap sits at just $119 million, and Outlook valuation highlights just how speculative the stock is ahead of this FDA decision. The market is really pricing in a positive outcome, and since the stock is so cheap in the mid-$2 range, it's seen a big run-up. As per Seeking Alpha, on a forward price to sales basis, they trade at 14.7x, versus a sector median of only 3.8x, which is almost a 291% premium. Similarly, its forward EV to sales ratio sits at 18.3x, compared with the sector's 3.5x, a staggering 430% premium. What this means in practice is that investors are already pricing in a lot of future growth and assuming Lytenava will generate meaningful revenue quite quickly. But because the company has essentially no current sales, the multiples are heavily inflated. It will leave the stock very sensitive to setbacks. However, assuming the FDA decision is positive, we should see the income from sales come in much sooner than expected. After all, Lytenava is a drug that's already in use in the UK and Europe. Those drug supplies and campaigns are already in place. It won't be as if Outlook is rolling out a completely new drug.

data.title: BlackRock TCP Capital: Still Too Speculative To Go Long
data.description:
In early 2024, I managed to identify some specific areas within BlackRock TCP Capital Corp. (TCPC) that were experiencing a notable buildup of risks. My main concern was linked to the weakening portfolio quality and emerging non-accruals. Even though the base dividend coverage was still relatively solid and the debt-to-equity level was just slightly above the 1.0x mark, considering the lack of a meaningful discount, I decided to rotate out of TCPC. Since then, TCPC has registered a negative total return of ~15% and a negative price return of almost 33%. At the same time, the BDC market (BIZD) has gone up. So, in terms of alpha generation, TCPC has been a disastrous investment.

However, what we have now is a heavily discounted TCPC that has already made some relatively deep dividend cuts. Currently, TCPC trades at an 18% discount to NAV, offering one of the highest dividend yields in the sector - 15.5%.

Given the backing of an institutional giant, BlackRock (BLK), it might theoretically be so that there is now an opportunity to pick TCPC up, while probably many investors have assumed a quite bearish stance against it (due to the recent underperformance and value destruction as I mentioned above).

Let's take a closer look at this case to see whether it is indeed an interesting BDC bargain to include in the portfolio mix.

Thesis review

To see what things look like for TCPC, it is worth exploring the recently published Q2 2025 financial data.

You will not like the start. The Q2 2025 adjusted NII per share came in at $0.31, which is $0.05 per share below the result achieved in the previous quarter. The main driver for the drop was clearly the reduction in the total investment income, where TCPC had to recognize full writedowns of several loans that were already labeled as underperforming ones. On top of these eliminations, there were four incremental non-accruals that TCPC had to recognize in Q2, which stimulated not only NII per share decline but also a clear decrease in the NAV statistic.

Apart from the TCPC-specific negatives, the BDC had to deal with the overall sector headwinds that are especially relevant for the middle and upper market first-lien players. Namely, the weighted average annual effective yield of TCPC's portfolio declined by 20 basis points on a quarter-to-quarter basis, landing at exactly 12.0% as of Q2 2025. And what really illustrates the issue is the fact that the new investments that were made during Q2 were underwritten at 10.8% yields - i.e., still a notable gap between the portfolio in-place yields and what can be sourced from the market now.

This portfolio vs. market yield difference is something that we just have to factor in when thinking about TCPC's future NII per share generation. There is nothing that TCPC can do to mitigate the issue (pressure) unless it changes the investment strategy from a relatively defensive one to, say, a second-lien focused one. From the new investment funding, we cannot draw such a conclusion. So, all things equal, TCPC's NII per share is set to go down.

Unfortunately, I have a couple of additional items, which, in my view, will also contribute to declining results.

First, the base rates are very likely trending downwards, already this September. As most of TCPC's loans are linked to floating rates, we can easily expect similar pressures as in the Q4 2024 - Q1 2025 period (i.e., a period when the BDCs experienced headwinds from the Fed interest rate cuts across the board).

Second, in early 2026, TCPC will have to roll over a $325 million fixed rate debt that has been sourced at 2.85% - a significant difference from the prevailing fixed rate financing rates. This will certainly inflate the interest expense component for TCPC.

Third, TCPC ended the Q2 at a debt-to-equity of 1.42x, which is definitely one of the highest leverage levels in the sector. It does not mean that TCPC will suffer additional value destruction from this fact alone, but it for sure implies that the growth prospects are very limited due to largely exhausted debt capacity.

Fourth, and this is quite important in the context of the current discount, TCPC's exposure to PIK interest income is considerable. In Q2 2025, about 11.4% of the total investment income came from PIK streams, which means that the actual cash generation for TCPC is much lower than what the adjusted NII per share indicates.

Fifth, the Q2 figures included a temporary boost from the partially waived base management fees that will be in place until Q3 2025 (inclusive). After Q3, we have to start calculating higher management fees.

Now, to be more balanced in my argument, I will also spell out some positives that I see here.

Besides the notable discount and rich dividend yield, the rate of change in both PIK and non-accruals has been encouraging. For example, the PIK share has declined by 20 basis points from the last quarter. Similarly, the non-accrual rate has also come down for the third quarter in a row - to 3.7% from 4.4% and 5.6% for Q1 2025 and Q4 2024, respectively (all measured on a fair value basis).

Another major positive for TCPC is that the current base dividend coverage is 124%, which puts the BDC in the sector's Top 10 statistic (in terms of dividend coverage strength). Plus, the excess cash flows have been directed to the share repurchases (i.e., 40,830 shares repurchased in Q2), which from the capital allocation perspective makes sense.

The bottom line

Now, if I put all of these puzzle pieces together, I have to say that TCPC remains an extremely speculative case. Even though the current situation looks somewhat acceptable - i.e., improving picture, deep discount, and high dividend coverage - the direction of travel in the adjusted NII per share generation is clearly negative. So, what we see in the broad statistics is only temporary and subject to deterioration going forward.

In this context, the 18% discount does not seem that attractive.

In my opinion, if the Fed cuts more than 50 basis points and/or there are more non-accruals coming online, then we will be most probably talking about yet another dividend cut from TCPC.

data.title: Walker And Dunlop Tries To Stay Healthy In A Struggling Market
data.description:
Walker and Dunlop (NYSE: WD) released its Q2 2025 earnings before the market opened on August 7. Prior to the announcement, the company’s stock had lost over 22% year-to-date, but the positive news from the commercial lender’s most recent quarter has given it a boost of about 13%. General market headwinds in commercial real estate have been challenging for WD over the last year, despite the company's previous decade of success, where it has experienced compound average growth in revenues of 12% and annual increases in net income of 18%.

Even with the strong recent earnings report, WD is trading well above its 10-year average PE, and the company may have some macro headwinds to battle in the final half of this year. The company has a strong record of previous success and an above average dividend yield. These things were not enough to persuade me to give the stock a Buy rating at this time.

Company Overview

Walker and Dunlop was founded in 1937 and is currently based in Bethesda, Maryland. The company is one of the world’s largest commercial real estate finance and advisory companies. Walker and Dunlop was the top lender for Fannie Mae’s Delegated Underwriting and Servicing program in 2024 and third in overall government-sponsored enterprise loans.

When reporting its financial results, Walker and Dunlop separates revenues and expenses into three segments – Capital Markets, Servicing and Asset Management, and Corporate. A description of each of these segments is as follows:

  • Capital Markets – Through this division, the company provides agency lending, debt brokerage, property sales, and appraisals. WD also provides real estate-related investment banking and advisory services.
  • Servicing and Asset Management – Activities for this segment include servicing the company’s loan portfolio as well as managing third-party capital invested in commercial real estate and tax credit equity funds.
  • Corporate – This division consists primarily of WD’s treasury operations and other corporate “support functions”.

With a market cap of $2.86 billion, WD ended last year with a loan servicing portfolio of over $135 billion, with $30 billion in debt origination in 2024. Last year, the company managed a transaction volume of $40 billion and brought in revenues of almost $1.1 billion. Of that revenue total, the largest share, about 52.2%, came from Servicing and Asset Management. Just over 46.3% came from Capital Markets.

WD has a dividend yield of 3.12% which is just slightly ahead of the median for its sector. Assuming it makes its payment in Q4, this will be the seventh consecutive year that the company has raised its payout. The five-year dividend growth rate for Walker and Dunlop is 14.9%.

Analysis of Walker and Dunlop

Analysis of the earnings for Walker and Dunlop is more challenging than for other companies because the bottom line for the company can be impacted significantly by the timing of various transactions. On first glance, the company’s diluted earnings per share have declined significantly over the last three years. After earning $6.36 per share in FY 2022, WD reported diluted profits of $3.18 per share in FY 2023 and $3.19 in FY 2024.

However, a closer look at some of the individual line items on the company’s consolidated financial statements paint a somewhat clearer, and more positive picture. Servicing fees have increased every year since 2022, starting at $300.2 million and ending at $325.6 million last year. Total revenues for Servicing & Asset Management were $507.4 million in FY 2022, but they grew to $563.8 million in FY 2023 and $591.6 million last year. The company has also been able to lower its overall expenses in the last three years, from a total of $607.4 million in 2022 when all three divisions were combined to $559.2 million in 2024.

Walker and Dunlop’s Capital Markets division was able to benefit greatly from the interest rate environment in 2022 as the company reported earnings of $4.64 per share in that business segment alone. In 2023 and 2024, Capital Markets combined to make only $3.19. These declines appear to be correcting themselves somewhat this year, while still not back at the levels seen in 2022. WD’s Capital Markets division reported diluted EPS of $0.97 in Q2 2025, a 194% improvement over the $0.33 earned in the same quarter last year. A complete breakdown of the results from the Capital Markets division from the most recent earnings announcement from the company can be seen below.

The other two divisions did not experience similar improvements, however. Servicing and Asset Management reported earnings of $1.10 per share after earning $1.19 in Q2 2024. The Corporate segment lost $1.08 per share after losing only $0.85 in the same quarter last year. For the Servicing division, the company reported lower fee income because of a reduction in the accrual for management fees from its low-income housing tax credit funds. The Corporate segment profits slipped because of an increase in personnel salaries and benefits.

The outlook for the company in the second half of 2025 is good, but not great. The company is expecting to record gains in the "high single-digits to double-digits" in diluted EPS. Adjusted core EPS, which removes non-recurring items, is expected to be flat to high-single digits even though the comparison has been negative in the first six months.

With any company that is heavily involved in commercial finance, debt should be an important part of an investment thesis. Walker and Dunlop’s ratings from S&P and Moody’s are straddling the line between investment grade and slightly speculative as shown below. The most recent outlook offered by both agencies is stable. On July 8 of this year, Fitch Ratings affirmed WD’s commercial primary servicer rating at CPS1-, which is a confirmation of the company’s servicing capability, not its creditworthiness. Again, the picture here looks good, but not great.

Market Outlook for Commercial Real Estate

Walker and Dunlop has an elevated exposure to commercial real estate and commercial mortgages. Multiple sources have published articles this year that predict an impending crisis for CRE lenders. Major issues of concern include increased default rates, decreased property values, and liquidity constraints. Because some of this news has been disseminated in the market for some time, I was surprised to find WD trading at such an elevated PE ratio when compared to its five-year average. The stock is trading below its price to book average, according to Morningstar, so the current share price is not quite as out of line as the PE ratio may imply.

Most concerning for me is that the market headwinds for Walker and Dunlop may get worse before they get better, putting further pressure on its valuation. The Mortgage Bankers Association noted an increase in delinquencies for commercial properties in Q1 of this year. Declines in economic growth in the second half of 2025 could cause further advancement in this trend.

In addition to delinquency concerns, commercial real estate is also dealing with higher interest rates and a wave of refinancing that is coming due in the next three years. Approximately $600 billion in commercial loans will mature on an annual basis between 2025 and 2028. Property insurance costs have risen, often faster than rental income. Vacancy rates for office properties remain high, with the average in most major cities ranging from 16 to 20%.

To its credit, Walker and Dunlop is trying to adjust to the demands of the market. Its Capital Markets division is expanding into new opportunities with hospitality and data centers. The company is also expanding into projects in Europe. While markets in Europe appear relatively healthy now, they have traditionally trailed the US in overall economic growth.

In its latest earnings report, the company also pointed out opportunity in multifamily housing, another key source for loan origination and servicing. With housing near all-time highs for adjusted costs, renting has become incredibly affordable on a relative basis. As of May of this year, renting offers a monthly savings of almost $540 per month over a typical home mortgage with principal and interest. This disparity should help keep occupancy rates high, increasing the quality and likelihood of payback for those loans.

Additional Risks to Consider

Walker and Dunlop relies heavily on Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac loan programs for origination volume. As I mentioned in my company overview, the company was the top lender for Fannie Mae’s DUS program and third in overall government-sponsored enterprise loans. Regulatory changes to these two programs could directly impact WD’s main sources of revenue. The Trump administration has announced that it may take both Fannie and Freddie public as soon as this year.

Walker and Dunlop’s CEO, Willy Walker, wrote about the potential consequences of this move back in March of this year. It is possible that privatization could cause a disruption in the mortgage market that would ultimately raise costs for borrowers. One estimation from Walker’s article stated that mortgage rates “could increase by 20-40 basis points due to uncertainty and new capital requirements”. He added, “We are concerned that a rushed privatization of Fannie Mae could undermine market confidence without an implicit or explicit government guarantee.” Additional risks for Walker and Dunlop from this scenario could include added pressure on margins, tighter rate spreads, and reduced market share.

The Bottom Line

My overall impression of Walker and Dunlop after concluding my research is that the company is well-managed based on its track record of success. My concerns about the current valuation of the stock and the headwinds that are still impacting its main sources of income are too much to push the needle over toward Buy for this stock at this time. I also think that the current debate about the privatization of Fannie and Freddie is worth watching as it relates to WD’s future business opportunities.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system appears to agree with my assessment of the company, giving the stock a “C-” in Valuation and a “D-” in Profitability. The stock has actually been in the Sell range for much of the last year. Further inspection into WD’s Valuation on Seeking Alpha reveals that the stock gets grades of “D” in PE Non-GAAP, PE GAAP, and PEG GAAP when compared to its sector.

data.title: Powell Pivot Sparks REIT Rebound
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets notched another series of record highs this week, surging into the weekend after surprisingly dovish commentary from Federal Reserve Chair Powell, who acknowledged the risk of the Fed's currently restrictive policy stance and hinted at a likely resumption of rate cuts next month. Powell used his final Jackson Hole speech as Fed Chair to deliver a clear policy pivot, stating “the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance" - an unexpected reversal after months of insistence that forecasted tariff-related inflation warranted a more hawkish approach. Markets were equally relieved by the policy-focused nature of Powell's speech amid speculation that the address may be used instead as a potential defiant sermon on central bank independence as a means to rebuke recent White House criticism.

Rallying after Powell's pivot to erase early-week declines, the S&P 500 climbed 0.2% this week, closing fractionally below fresh all-time record-highs. The gains were more pronounced across the smaller-cap benchmarks, which have been lagging their large-cap, tech-heavy peers to a historic degree since the start of the Fed's hiking cycle in early 2022. The Small-Cap 600 surged 3.4% this week, while the Mid-Cap 400 gained 2.6%, far outpacing the mega-cap Nasdaq 100, which declined 1.0%. Real estate equities - the most "Fed sensitive" market segment - naturally led the gains this week, as rate relief appears to finally be in sight after three years of battling stiff headwinds. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.8% this week, with 17-of-18 property sectors in positive territory, while the Mortgage REIT Index advanced 2.1%. Homebuilders and the broader Housing Index also delivered strong gains as 30-Year Mortgage Rates dipped to the lowest levels since last October.

Benchmark interest rates tumbled during and after Powell's speech as traders interpreted the Chairman's focus on employment risks and comments on "adjusting" the policy stance as a clear signal that the Fed is leaning towards a rate cut at its mid-September meeting. Dipping to four-month lows, the policy-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield fell by 6 basis points this week to 3.69% - down sharply from the recent highs of 3.95% late last month. Providing some added support at the long end of the curve, S&P Global Ratings affirmed its "AA+" credit rating on the U.S. this week, noting the "meaningful" revenue generated from new tariffs will help to offset the fiscal hit from recent tax and spending legislation. The 10-Year Treasury Yield dipped by 6 basis points this week to close at 4.25%, trading toward the lower end of its three-month range between 4.20% in early August and 4.60% in mid-May. Swaps markets now imply a Fed Funds Rate of 3.95% at the end of 2025 (vs. 4.50% now), implying 2.2 Fed interest rate cuts in 2025 - roughly unchanged on the week. The odds of a September rate cut, meanwhile, stand at roughly 83%. Meanwhile, WTI Crude Oil rebounded from two-month lows this week after the meeting between President Trump and Russian President Putin failed to yield an immediate breakthrough in peace negotiations.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Industrial: Are the animal spirits starting to come alive in the real estate sector? Plymouth Industrial (PLYM) - which we've owned in the REIT Dividend Growth Portfolio - soared 41% this week after it received a takeover offer from asset manager Sixth Street Partners, which offered to buy PLYM for $24.10 per share in cash - a 65% premium over its prior closing price. PLYM - a small-cap industrial REIT with a market cap of approx $650M before today's surge - owns 148 properties comprising over 30M square feet, primarily in the Midwest and Southeastern U.S., dubbed the "golden triangle." CEO Jeffrey Witherell joined the Hoya Hotseat earlier this year to discuss PLYM's strategy. Sixth Street already owns 10% of PLYM common shares and owns additional interest via its strategic partnership announced last year, in which it provided $250M to PLYM in exchange for preferred stock and a joint venture interest in its Chicago portfolio - a deal that was criticized by analysts for its complexity and cost. PLYM - which went public in 2017 - had generally been performing in line with its industrial-focused peers over a 3-year and 5-year basis, but had underperformed since the partnership was announced last August. Plymouth announced that its board plans to evaluate the offer with its advisers to determine whether the deal serves shareholders’ interests.

Healthcare: Ventas (VTR) - the second-largest senior housing REIT - traded flat this week after Bloomberg reported that it will acquire a $600M portfolio of six Long Island-based senior communities from Harrison Street Asset Management - its largest transaction this year. Senior housing operator B2K - which operates 24 senior housing facilities under The Bristal brand - will continue to manage the properties and can earn incentive fees based on performance. Ventas CIO Justin Hutchens confirmed to Bloomberg, noting the portfolio is comprised of "high-quality senior living communities with upside potential." Ventas and its larger peer Welltower (WELL) have been among the most active REITs on the acquisitions front during this past three-year "bear market" for commercial real estate, a time that has seen historically low levels of REIT acquisition activity. Including this recent deal, Ventas has acquired nearly $2 billion in senior housing properties YTD, while Welltower announced last month that it has acquired $9.2 billion in properties YTD - combined accounting for 42% of the $26.0B in total acquisitions across the entire REIT sector this year. Riding the post-pandemic senior housing revival, VTR and WELL are two of the only REITs whose shares have traded at sizable and consistent premiums to their consensus Net Asset Value ("NAV") in recent years - a critical "cost of capital" gauge enabling accretive acquisitions.

Mortgage: Residential mREIT Two Harbors (TWO) declined 3% this week after it announced that it agreed to pay $375M in cash to settle long-running litigation related to the April 2020 termination of its external manager Pine River Capital Management ("PRCM"). Back in 2020, TWO's Board voted to terminate its external management agreement with PRCM "for cause," citing material breaches and gross negligence, thus avoiding a roughly $140M termination fee. The termination came after TWO had plunged more than 70% over the preceding month amid the early pandemic turmoil, worse than the 50% plunge in the broader Mortgage REIT Index in that time. A five-year legal battle ensued, with PRCM seeking payment of the fee with interest plus excess damages related to claims that TWO poached its employees and stole its intellectual property. In May, a judge found that Two Harbors did not have a basis to terminate the agreement for cause, prompting TWO to record a $198.9M contingency liability to cover the termination fee. While the $375M settlement was on the higher-end of analyst estimates, it was below the nearly $500M in damages that PRCM was seeking. In exchange for the payment, Pine River will relinquish all ownership claims to IP, ending the long-running dispute. TWO noted that after the payment, its book value per share ("BVPS") is $11.06 per common share, still roughly 12% above its closing share price.

Alongside the announced settlement, Two Harbors (TWO) also announced that it would reduce its dividend for the second time this year - one of sixteen REITs that have lowered their dividends this year. TWO declared a $0.34/share quarterly dividend (13.4% dividend yield), a 13% decrease from its prior dividend of $0.39. On the upside this week, industrial REIT Eastgroup Properties (EGP) rallied 4% after raising its quarterly dividend by 11% to $1.55/share (3.7% dividend yield), becoming the 54th REIT to raise its dividend this year.
